California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
August 30, 1988 
Honorable Michael D. Bradbury 
District Attorney 
County of Ventura 
Hall of Justice 
800 South Victoria Ave. 
Ventura, CA 
93009 
Dear Mr. Bradbury: 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-88-28l 
This is in response to your letter requesting advice 
regarding application of the new restrictions on 
publicly-funded mass mailings under Proposition 73. 
Enclosed are copies of our first advice letters 
interpreting the mass mailing restrictions, and the emergency 
regulation adopted by the Commission on July 26, 1988. 
After 
examining the new statute and the analysis by the Legislative 
Analyst, we have concluded that the voters intended to restrict 
mailings by state and local elected officials, but did not 
intend to ban publicly funded mailings entirely. 
Upon review of the materials you have submitted to us, we 
conclude that each of these documents qualifies as a "mass 
mailing" if sent to two-hundred or more individuals in a single 
calendar month. 
(Emergency Regulation l890l(a).) 
Because each 
of the documents features your name, picture, or signature, all 
of these publications are prohibited under the mass mailing 
restrictions of Proposition 73. 
If you have any questions regarding this response, please 
contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Dear Ms. Griffiths: 
10 
V' 
.. -
This office routinely mails several publications and 
miscellaneous correspondence regarding district attorney 
programs. 
I have included three such publications/letters as 
examples: 
A Biennial Report (previously an annual report was 
issued), a Victim's Newsletter, and a letter to business people 
who utilize our Non-Sufficient Fund Check Restitution Program. 
Please provide me with your opinion whether these 
publications/correspondence fall within the mass mailing 
prohibitions of Government Code sections 82041.5 and 89001. 
If you need additional information, please contact Assistant 
District Attorney Colleen Toy White at (805) 654-2523. 
Thank you. 
Very truly yours, 
District Attorney

• 
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Michael D. Bradbury 
District Attorney 
County of Ventura 
Hall of Justice 
800 South Victoria Avenue 
Ventura, CA 
93009 
Dear Mr. Bradbury: 
July 21, 1988 
Re: 
88-281 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform 
Act was received on July 19, 1988 by the Fair Political 
Practices Commission. 
If you have any questions about your' 
advice request, you may contact Lilly spitz, an attorney in the 
Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, 
or more information is needed, you should expect a response 
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written 
advice. 
If more information is needed, the person assigned to 
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to 
advise you as to information needed. 
If your request is for 
informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we can. 
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 
18329).) 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Vol. 2,No. 1 
January 1988 
Domestic Violence: 
a national problem 
INSIDE 
MADD 
honors 
Victim 
Advocate 
Special 
unit helps 
parents find 
children 
Money 
recovered 
for crime 
victims 
Featuring: 
Saundra 
Brewer 
p.2 
p.3 
p.4 
p.5 
I
f it is true that violence begets 
violence, then nowhere does it 
have a more dramatic effect than 
in the home. 
• One out of every two American 
families experience at least one 
incident of domestic violence. 
• Every 17 seconds in the United 
States, an incident of violence 
occurs against a woman by her 
partner. Yet, domestic violence 
might be the largest under-reported 
crime in America. 
• Most people, when beaten by 
someone in their own family 
feel afraid, embarrassed"guilty 
and helpless. 
Violence-prone families are, 
from all outward appearances, no 
different from average American 
families. They can be found 
among all socioeconomic levels, 
and in every city and town in 
Ventura County; but domestic 
violence is not a local or isolated 
phenomenon. It is a national 
problem. 
California's Office of Criminal 
Justice Planning annually 
distributes over $4.2 million in 
federal and state funds to 
domestic violence projects 
throughout the state. The office 
provides funds for direct 
assistance; the development of 
model domestic violence 
prevention programs; and pilot 
programs that give employment 
counseling, training and 
placement assistance. 
The Ventura County District 
Attorney's Office has victim 
advocates who help victims obtain 
Michael D. 
Bradbury. 
District 
Attorney 
"I consider 
family violence 
a serious social 
problem de-
manding the 
a~ntion of tbe 
Ventura County, Calif. 
entire Criminal Justice System. Domestic 
violence is one of the leading sources 
of criminal homicide in general, and 
officer injury and death, in particular. 
It is the policy of my office to file and 
prosecute all provable cases involving 
domestic violence. I oppose attempts 
to divert or otherwise excuse any 
violent offender from criminal 
responsibility. " 
law enforcement, legal assistance, 
and social services. Advocates 
also help victims of domestic 
violence apply for temporary 
restraining orders. When 
necessary, the advocates give 
court support. 
Since October 1986, the 
Victim/Witness Division has 
helped 1,107 victims obtain 
. temporary restraining orders. 
In Ventura County, local agencies 
such as Interface, The Coalition 
Against Household Violence, Child 
Abuse and Neglect, and the Rape and 
Sexual Abuse Center, also provide 
immediate safety and ongoing support 
for victims of violence and abuse.

sections.question: Please provide me with your opinion whether these publications/correspondence fall within the mass mailing prohibitions of Government Code sections 82041.5 and 89001.
sections.conclusion: Upon review of the materials you have submitted to us, we conclude that each of these documents qualifies as a "mass mailing" if sent to two-hundred or more individuals in a single calendar month. Because each of the documents features your name, picture, or signature, all of these publications are prohibited under the mass mailing restrictions of Proposition 73.
sections.question_synthetic: Do the District Attorney's three publications (Biennial Report, Victim's Newsletter, and NSF Check Restitution Program letter) qualify as prohibited mass mailings under Proposition 73?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that all three documents would qualify as prohibited mass mailings under Proposition 73 if sent to 200+ individuals in a single calendar month, as they each feature the District Attorney's name, picture, or signature.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a complete advice letter file with both the incoming request (dated July 18, 1988) and FPPC response (dated August 30, 1988), plus initial receipt letter (July 21, 1988) and all three sample publications referenced. Question and conclusion sections are clearly identifiable from the August 30 response letter. High confidence extraction with complete documentation trail.

QUESTION: Please provide me with your opinion whether these publications/correspondence fall within the mass mailing prohibitions of Government Code sections 82041.5 and 89001.

CONCLUSION: Upon review of the materials you have submitted to us, we conclude that each of these documents qualifies as a "mass mailing" if sent to two-hundred or more individuals in a single calendar month. Because each of the documents features your name, picture, or signature, all of these publications are prohibited under the mass mailing restrictions of Proposition 73.

embedding.summary: The District Attorney of Ventura County sought FPPC advice on whether three routine office publications (Biennial Report, Victim's Newsletter, and NSF Check letter) violate Proposition 73's mass mailing restrictions. The FPPC advised that all three documents are prohibited mass mailings if distributed to 200+ individuals monthly, because they feature the District Attorney's name, picture, or signature.
